content.full_text:
May 30, 1978

Mr. William J. Brunick
Welebir and Brunick
Suite 410, Santa Fe Federal Plaza
330 North "D" Street
Post Office Box 351
San Bernardino, California 92402

Dear Mr. Brunick:

Thank you for your letter of April 17, 1978, regarding contributions
by the San Bernardino Public Employees Association.

The jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices Commission is limited
to matters arising under the Political Reform Act, and it is not
possible for us to advise you about the tax consequences of contribu-
tions by a non-profit organization. You should seek such advice from
the Franchise Tax Board.

Your second question requests information about the reporting re-
quirements of the Political Reform Act. It is not possible to give
a definite answer without some detailed information about the cir-
cumstances in the case you present.

Generally speaking, if a membership organization, such as the Public
Employees Association here, makes a contribution and has never made
such political contributions in the past, it would not incur a re-
porting obligation until it qualified as a major donor by contributing
a total amount of at least $5,000.

If, however, the organization has made contributions in the past,
then the members of the organization would know or have reason to
know that a portion of their dues or membership fees would be spent
for political purposes. In such a case the organization would have
to earmark a certain reasonable portion of its receipts for political
purposes and report that amount as a contribution received. If the
amount earmarked equals or exceeds $500 the organization will become
a committee and incur a reporting obligation. If any member's share
of the amount raised for political purposes equals or exceeds $50,
that member must be individually identified as a contributor to the
organization.

In addition, any funds raised from the members specifically for
political purposes would be counted toward the $500 threshold above.

If the organization has never made political contributions in the
past, so that the $5,000 threshold applies, it would report on
Form 461.

27

Mr. William J. Brunick
May 30, 1978
Page Two

If the organization has made political contributions in the past, so
that the $500 threshold applies, or if the organization raises $500
or more specifically for political purposes it will report on either
Form 450 or Form 420. The Form 420 must be used if any source of the
organization's political fund provides an amount which equals or
exceeds $50. Otherwise, Form 450 may be used.

Reporting deadlines in connection with the June 6, 1978, primary
election are April 30, May 25, and July 31, 1978; the respective
closing dates are April 23, May 22, and June 30, 1978.

Without precise information about the organization you represent it
is impossible to provide more than this general sketch of the reporting
requirements. If you have additional questions, you may call me at
(916) 322-5562.

Sincerely,


John D. Giansello
Political Reform Analyst
Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division

WELEBLR & BRUNICK

ATTORNEYS AT LAW

DOUGLAS P. WELEBLR                    SUITE 410 SANTA FE FEDERAL PLAZA                    202 REDLANDS PLAZA
WILLIAM J. BRUNICK                     330 NORTH "D" STREET                                 2 EAST CITRUS
JAMES B. CASE                          POST OFFICE BOX 151                                  REDLANDS, CALIFORNIA 92373
ROBERT H. RIMBACH                      SAN BERNARDINO, CALIFORNIA 92402                     TELEPHONE 793-0816

                                       TELEPHONE AREA CODE 714
                                       (884-6205  886-6839
                                       824-0623                                             PLEASE REFER TO FILE NO.


                                       April 17, 1978




Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 8C7
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: John D. Giansello

Dear Mr. Giansello:

This office represents the San Bernardino Public Employees
Association. This organization is an IRC 501(c)(5) and
State of California 23701(a) exempt organization.

Recently request has been made from this organization to
contribute funds for the campaign against the passage of the
Jarvis Amendment.

Recent statements from the Franchise Tax Board indicate that
such contributions from a nonprofit organization might jeopardize
the tax status of the corporation. Also, if such contributions
are made, what sort of reporting requirements need be made
pursuant to the Political Reform Act of 1974?

Any information you might be able to provide would be appreciated.

                                       Yours very truly,

                                       WELEBLR & BRUNICK



                                       William J. Brunick

WJB:dm
